examples.title: Simple RPC Client-Server Pseudocode
examples.description: Demonstrates how RPC provides access transparency by making remote calls appear local
examples.code:
# Server Side (Service Provider)
def remote_add(a, b):
 return a + b

# Client Side (Service Requestor)
# Using stub to make remote call look local
result = stub.remote_add(5, 3) # Appears local, but actually sends RPC
print(result) # Output: 8
examples.explanation: The stub at client side marshals parameters, sends request to server, waits for response, and unmarshals the result. This provides access transparency - the programmer writes normal function call syntax while the middleware handles all distributed communication.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) where m is message size

examples.title: Heartbeat-based Failure Detection
examples.description: Simple algorithm to detect node failures in distributed system
examples.code:
BEGIN
 CONSTANT heartbeat_interval = 5 seconds
 CONSTANT timeout_threshold = 3
 
 FUNCTION monitor_nodes():
 FOR each node in active_nodes:
 IF current_time - node.last_heartbeat > heartbeat_interval * timeout_threshold:
 mark node as FAILED
 initiate failover_protocol(node)
 ELSE
 node.status = HEALTHY
 
 FUNCTION receive_heartbeat(node_id):
 active_nodes[node_id].last_heartbeat = current_time
 active_nodes[node_id].status = HEALTHY
END
examples.explanation: Each monitored node sends periodic heartbeats. If no heartbeat arrives within timeout_threshold × interval, the node is considered failed. This handles partial failures - the monitor continues even if some nodes fail. Trade-off: longer timeouts reduce false positives but delay failure detection.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of nodes monitored

examples.title: Lamport Timestamp for Event Ordering
examples.description: Demonstrates logical clock to order events without global clock
examples.code:
BEGIN
 GLOBAL counter = 0
 
 FUNCTION lamport_timestamp():
 counter = counter + 1
 RETURN counter
 
 FUNCTION send_message(message):
 message.timestamp = lamport_timestamp()
 send(message)
 
 FUNCTION receive_message(message):
 counter = max(counter, message.timestamp) + 1
 process(message)
 
 # Each process maintains local counter
 # Events are partially ordered by timestamps
 # This enables causality tracking without global clock
END
examples.explanation: Lamport timestamps provide causal ordering of events in distributed systems. Each event increments a logical counter. When receiving a message with timestamp T, the receiver sets its counter to max(local, T) + 1. This creates a partial order reflecting causal relationships without requiring synchronized physical clocks.
